description: Ambleside Online Year 3 — Charlotte Mason living books curriculum for grades 2-3. Introduction to longer novels, biography, and Plutarch's lives. Richer Socratic discussion and written narration begins.

books.title: Little Women
books.author: Louisa May Alcott
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: A rich character study ideal for discussing virtue and the four temperaments. Each sister embodies different strengths and weaknesses. 'Which sister are you most like and why?'

books.title: The Merry Adventures of Robin Hood
books.author: Howard Pyle
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: Pyle's prose is deliberately archaic — read aloud slowly. Excellent for discussing justice vs. law. 'Was Robin Hood right to steal from the rich? Why or why not?'

books.title: The Swiss Family Robinson
books.author: Johann David Wyss
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: The family's gratitude to God for provision runs throughout. Excellent natural history woven in — many animals the children encounter can be researched in nature study.

books.title: Plutarch's Lives (Ambleside selections, North translation)
books.author: Plutarch / Thomas North
books.subject: history
books.notes: Charlotte Mason considered Plutarch's Lives essential. Use the Ambleside-selected passages. Read slowly, discuss each moral dilemma. 'What made Publicola a good leader?' Written narration begins here.

books.title: Parables from Nature
books.author: Margaret Gatty
books.subject: nature_study
books.notes: Nature stories with rich theological and moral depth — in the tradition of Charlotte Mason's nature-faith integration. Ask: 'What does the caterpillar's transformation teach us?'

books.title: Our Island Story
books.author: Henrietta Elizabeth Marshall
books.subject: history
books.notes: Narrative English history from the Romans to Queen Victoria. Marshall writes history as living story. One chapter per session — each is self-contained with a clear hero or crisis.

books.title: Trial and Triumph: Stories from Church History
books.author: Richard Hannula
books.subject: saints
books.notes: Continue or complete this resource. By Year 3 the child may begin written narration of a saint's story. 'Write three sentences telling why this person was brave.'

books.title: The Wind in the Willows
books.author: Kenneth Grahame
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: If not read in Year 2, excellent supplemental here. Grahame's prose lends itself to written narration of the descriptions — 'Describe the River Bank in your own words.'

books.title: Secrets of the Woods
books.author: William J. Long
books.subject: nature_study
books.notes: If not completed in Year 1. Pair with nature notebook entries after each chapter — the child draws and describes the animal observed in the story.

books.title: A Child's History of the World
books.author: V. M. Hillyer
books.subject: history
books.notes: Complete if not finished in earlier years. By Year 3, add timeline entries as each chapter is read — a visual history spine grows with the child.

books.title: Burgess Bird Book for Children
books.author: Thornton W. Burgess
books.subject: nature_study
books.notes: Burgess presents bird facts through story — Peter Rabbit learns about birds from Old Mother Nature. Children absorb real ornithology within a narrative frame.

books.title: The Stars: A New Way to See Them
books.author: H. A. Rey
books.subject: science
books.notes: Rey's stargazing guide makes constellations memorable through redrawn shapes. Best in Term 3 for evening observation. Connect to the wonder of creation: 'God calls each star by name.'
